sections.assessment: This chapter has a voice — that much is undeniable. It is absolutely consistent from the first sentence to the last, and consistency is the foundational requirement. The prose speaks in a deliberate, formal, almost liturgical register that mirrors the ecclesiastical world it inhabits, and that is a genuine craft achievement. But King's core test for voice is not consistency alone: it is personality. The narrator of this chapter has no opinions, no editorializing, no asides, no sense of a person behind the prose who chose to tell you this story rather than some other story. The narrator is a style, not a person. King's narrators in third person — think the wide-angle voice of 'The Stand' or the sardonic asides threading through 'It' — carry attitude. They will say things like 'God makes the world go round, but money grease the wheels' or 'the most important things are the hardest to say.' This narrator says 'The cantor was the cantor.' That is the line that most perfectly exposes the problem: it sounds like depth and is actually nothing. The repetitive syntactic tic — 'on the present accounting,' 'on Augustine's report from the freedman,' 'on the long custom of the African church' — accumulates past the point of register and into the territory of mannerism. Mannerism is what King means by literary affectation: the move that calls attention to itself rather than to the story. The voice earns enormous credit for its final sentence — 'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold' — which is nine words doing the work of a paragraph, stripped clean, and devastating. That sentence has a storyteller behind it. The chapter needs more of that person and less of the repeated syntactic architecture that, by the fourth or fifth iteration, has become wallpaper.
good_examples: 'He did not move because the body of a man at thirty-six who had been pointed at across the nave of a basilica he had not been baptised in does not, in the first count, move.' — The voice finds genuine strangeness and psychological truth here; the construction earns its awkwardness.

'He had not thought about Hippo.' — A single sentence paragraph after a long interior passage. The narrator steps on the brake at exactly the right moment. This is personality expressed through structure.

'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold.' — The best sentence in the chapter. Understated, precise, loaded. The narrator trusts the reader completely. King would underline this one.

'He wept. / He did not weep loudly.' — The two-sentence pivot works. The second sentence reframes the first and does it without commentary. The voice is at its least affected here and therefore most powerful.
bad_examples: 'The Lenten readings were the readings proper to the third Sunday. The cantor was the cantor.' — 'The cantor was the cantor' is pure literary affectation. It impersonates profundity while delivering zero information. King would cross it out in red and write 'SO WHAT?' in the margin.

'He did not — and Augustine would, in the years to come, remember this with a particular respect for the deacon's restraint — he did not pull.' — The editorial interruption is the right instinct (this is the narrator trying to editorialize) but the construction is so knotted it kills the moment. The restraint being noted deserved its own clean sentence, not a dash-parenthetical inside the action.

The phrase 'on the present accounting' appears at least seven times; 'on Augustine's report' or variants appear four or five times. By the third iteration the reader has clocked it as a tic. A voice tic is not a voice — it is a voice substitute. King's rule: if you notice a pet phrase, kill it on sight after one or two uses.

'He understood, at the rail, that the carrying his mother had given him at Ostia had been, on her own honest accounting, the carrying of the thing in the box; and that the carrying of the thing in the box had been, on her implicit instruction which he had not, in three years and four months, finished hearing, the carrying of whatever further work the office of a man of his disposition would require him to take...' — This sentence runs six lines and tells us almost nothing new. The voice has disappeared into syntax. King: 'The road to hell is paved with adverbs' — but also with subordinate clauses piled six deep.
suggestions: Give the narrator one opinion. Just one, stated plainly and without apology. Let the narrator tell us directly what Tigisius's pointing across the nave cost Augustine in the body — not in formal clauses about 'the body of a man at thirty-six' but in a single declarative line with an attitude behind it. The narrator knows things. Let them say something they know.

Audit every 'on the present accounting' / 'on the long custom' / 'on Augustine's report' construction and cut at least two-thirds of them. Replace the weakest ones with concrete sensory detail — what does the nave smell like at the third hour? What does the old bishop's voice actually sound like in that imperfect Latin? Ground the voice in the world rather than in its own syntax.

The chapter is labeled a thriller. The voice does not know this. Find two moments where the narrator can generate velocity — shorter sentences, punched paragraphs, the narrator leaning in. The ordination sequence should be breathless. Right now it reads like a chronicle. Let the voice break its own formal pattern at the moment of highest stakes and the formality will mean more when it returns.

'The cantor was the cantor' must be cut or replaced. If the intent is to show the ordinary mass proceeding around an extraordinary event, show it through one specific, odd sensory detail — a child in the congregation scratching his knee, a candle guttering near the east window — not through a tautology that signals literary seriousness without delivering it.

The ending is the model for the whole chapter. 'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold.' Write that sentence, then work backward through the chapter and ask: which paragraphs earned this sentence? Cut the ones that didn't. The voice that wrote that closing line is the voice this chapter needs.

sections.assessment: This chapter operates in two registers that are in perpetual tension with each other: a cool, liturgical-chronicle voice that narrates events with the formal remove of a church record, and rare but genuine bursts of interiority that show what the whole chapter could be. The dominant register is far psychic distance. We watch Augustine from across the nave, which is exactly wrong — we should be behind his eyes, not across the room from him. The prose's deliberate syntactic rhythm ('on the X of Y,' 'who had been,' 'who would') creates a formal, distancing surface even as the content is about a man's interior world collapsing in real time. The author knows what deep POV looks like: the three consecutive direct-thought sentences at the climax are excellent, the cold calculation about what refusing would cost is real interior reckoning, and 'He had not thought about Hippo' is devastating in its simplicity. But these islands of closeness are surrounded by sea of chronicle. King's test is this: can the reader feel the hands on Augustine's arms — the specific heat, the pressure, the way the body registers before the mind catches up? Can the reader feel the room go cold when a finger points across a nave? This chapter tells us what Augustine experienced. It does not yet make us experience it with him. The prose style itself — however deliberate and impressive as a historical aesthetic — is structurally the enemy of deep POV, and that is the central problem.
good_examples: 'He thought: the man I am about to become is not the man I have been planning to become. He thought: the man I have been planning to become is not the man my mother was watching me become for thirty-three years. He thought: my mother knew this hour was coming.' — Three direct-thought sentences with no filtering, no 'he thought that' construction. Present-tense impact. This is what the whole chapter should sound like.

'He had not thought about Hippo.' — Four words. After the long interior inventory of what he HAD been thinking about, this lands like a door closing. Perfect close POV. King would frame this on his wall.

'I have been the watcher. You will be the carrier. The carrying is the work. There is no other work that comes with it.' — The mother's words rendered as direct speech inside the memory. Pulls us into Augustine's skull viscerally, bypasses narration entirely.

'He understood, in the count, that the call was not a call he could refuse... the saying of no, at this hour in this basilica with this congregation behind him on its feet, was a saying that would, on every accounting of his future life...' — The most sustained interior monologue in the chapter. We're inside the calculus of a man who sees every door closing simultaneously. The syntax here earns its complexity.
'He wept.' — Two words after the long liturgical build. The restraint is King-level. Everything that follows — the explanation of the weeping — could be cut, but the two words cannot.
bad_examples: 'the same Tigisius who would, six years later, bring the news to Augustine in his own cell at the bishop's residence about the Donatist preacher named Crispus who had begun to be heard in the harbour district.' — Narrator intrusion from outside Augustine's POV and outside Augustine's time. In this moment, Augustine has no access to Tigisius's future. This aside steps entirely out of the character's head, kills the scene's tension, and reads like a footnote. It belongs nowhere near this beat.

'He did not move because the body of a man at thirty-six who had been pointed at across the nave of a basilica he had not been baptised in does not, in the first count, move.' — The analytical construction removes us from the experience at the exact moment the experience should hit hardest. We're getting the post-hoc explanation. We should be inside the freeze: no breath, no thought, just the finger pointing and the blood going wrong in his chest.

'Augustine had not, on coming in, taken any particular note of the bishop's chair at the cathedra, except to mark — in the small habitual marking that any monastic visitor performed on entering an unfamiliar basilica — that the cathedra was occupied...' — Reported observation with explanatory parenthetical. 'He saw,' 'he noticed,' 'he marked' are filtering words. Cut the scaffolding and put us in the observation itself.

'The bishop of Hippo was Valerius. He was Greek by birth and had been at Hippo in the chair for nine years. His Latin was the Latin of a man who had learned it in his thirties.' — This is backstory delivered as dossier. Whose knowledge is this? It doesn't feel like Augustine thinking it in the moment. It feels like a narrator briefing the reader. Even if you want this information here, it needs to be colored by Augustine's specific response to what he's seeing and hearing, not delivered clinically from outside.
suggestions: Cut the Tigisius future-tense aside entirely. If his identity needs establishing, do it in Augustine's present-tense perception of the man — what Augustine sees, what he recognizes, nothing more.

The opening two pages are backstory-as-chronicle with no interiority. Compress them to one paragraph or convert them to present-moment thought: Augustine in the side aisle, the mass beginning, his mind briefly on the freedman. Start us inside his head, not in the records office.

When the congregation touches Augustine — their hands on his arms and shoulders and back — this is the scene's most physical moment and the prose gives us nothing. Give us the specific heat of a stranger's palm. His breath going shallow. The nave tilting. The body before the mind.

Replace 'He did not move because the body of a man at thirty-six...' with the actual experience of not moving. What does frozen feel like from inside? King would give us the physical fact before the explanation, if the explanation appears at all.

The direct-thought format — 'He thought: *italics*' — works brilliantly at the climax. Seed it earlier. When Tigisius points across the nave, when the congregation rises, when they begin moving him forward: what does Augustine think, in the raw unfinished way people think in crisis? Not 'he understood' or 'he felt' — the thought itself, ugly and incomplete.

Audit every instance of 'on Augustine's report,' 'on the freedman's report,' 'on his own honest accounting,' and similar constructions. These are chronicle-distancing phrases that place a narrator between the reader and the character. Some can stay as period voice; most should be cut or converted to direct thought.

The final image — 'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold' — is strong, but the chapter ends with action, not interiority. Give us one last sentence from inside: what does cold mean to him at this specific hour? Let the reader leave his skull, not the room.

sections.assessment: This chapter has a genuinely accomplished dramatic engine — the involuntary ordination by popular acclamation is King-grade pressure, and several moments reveal character through action with real precision. The deacon Tigisius not pulling Augustine's arm, the body that 'does not, in the first count, move,' the quiet weeping after the rite — these work because they show us who this man is from the outside in, which is the right direction. But the chapter repeatedly undermines itself with the thing King most despises: backstory delivered as report. The opening three paragraphs are a pre-scene inventory that explains why Augustine is in Hippo before we are allowed to care that he is. The interior monologue while the congregation is physically moving him to the rail — at the single most dramatic moment in the chapter — stops the action cold to deliver a three-year biographical summary including the dead son, the woman in Carthage, the monastic house, the writing projects. King would say: you've got a hundred and forty Christians carrying a man to a rail, and you're talking about his correspondence habits. The 'carrying' motif is genuinely interesting and the mother's final quoted words land — but the apparatus explaining what the carrying means is over-engineered and over-repeated. The chapter trusts the reader less than it should.
good_examples: 'He did not — and Augustine would, in the years to come, remember this with a particular respect for the deacon's restraint — he did not pull.' — Character defined by a single restrained action. We learn Tigisius and Augustine simultaneously: what one man does, what the other notices and values. This is textbook King.

'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold.' — Perfect final image. Specific, mysterious, earned. Every word doing work. This is the kind of sentence that makes a reader go back and read the whole chapter again.

'He wept. He did not weep loudly.' — The short declarative followed by the qualification. Action, not explanation. And then the gap between how the congregation reads it and what it actually is — that's character revealed through the distinction between public and private truth.

'The plan has changed. I will not be returning to Tagaste with you.' — No emotional throat-clearing. The dialogue is compressed and direct, and the restraint of it reveals more about who Augustine is than any of the interior monologue passages do.
'They moved Augustine forward... He did not, in the moving, resist.' — The passive construction here is exactly right. Augustine being moved rather than moving. Character through grammar.
bad_examples: 'The man was a friend of his at Tagaste, a freedman who had been making, for the better part of a year, the small slow approaches of a man who was thinking about leaving the world...' — This is a resume. Three paragraphs of scene-setting before the scene begins. King would cut all of it and start in the basilica. The freedman's history can come out through action, through the conversation at the end of the chapter, through a glance during the homily — not as a pre-scene briefing.

'He had thought about the writing he was doing on the matter of the will and the matter of the soul and the matter of the music. He had thought about his son, who had died in the late summer of 389 at Tagaste at the age of seventeen...' — This biographical inventory arrives during the most physically charged moment in the chapter — Augustine is being carried by a crowd. Stopping here to deliver three years of backstory is like cutting to a flashback during a car crash. Wrong moment, wrong method.

'the same Tigisius who would, six years later, bring the news to Augustine in his own cell at the bishop's residence about the Donatist preacher named Crispus who had begun to be heard in the harbour district' — This flash-forward is a historical footnote in a dramatic scene. It reads like the author reassuring themselves that they know more than they're showing. Cut it entirely.

'His Latin was the Latin of a man who had learned it in his thirties. His preaching was, on Augustine's report from the freedman who had been at Hippo for the previous year, the preaching of a man who had something to say...' — This is resume characterization. Valerius speaks in this chapter. Let his actual Latin on the page do the work. Show us the labored construction, the careful choosing of words. Don't tell us what Augustine heard secondhand from the freedman.

'He understood, at the rail, that the carrying his mother had given him at Ostia had been, on her own honest accounting, the carrying of the thing in the box; and that the carrying of the thing in the box had been, on her implicit instruction which he had not, in three years and four months, finished hearing...' — This over-explains the mystery it's trying to protect. The mother's actual quoted words are strong. The architecture of explanation surrounding them is not. Trust the reader to carry the word 'carrying' without this much scaffolding.
suggestions: Cut the first three paragraphs entirely. Start the chapter with Augustine taking his place in the side aisle, already in the mass. The reason he is in Hippo can come out naturally — a line of dialogue with the freedman at the end, or a single sentence of interior thought in a quiet moment. Earned context, not pre-scene briefing.

When the congregation moves Augustine to the rail, cut everything from 'He did not resist because...' through '...twelve by the end of the year.' All of it. That biographical inventory belongs in the vestry scene, where Augustine sits alone on the bench. That is the right moment for his past to come forward — after the action, in the quiet. Restore the momentum of the crowd.
Cut the Tigisius flash-forward entirely. If Tigisius matters to the series, let him appear again in a later chapter and earn his history there.

Let Valerius's 'bad Latin' show in the actual texture of his speech on the page. Right now his homily is rendered in clean, elegant English. Give it something — a slightly off-register construction, an overly careful word choice, a pause where a native speaker wouldn't pause. Characterize through the prose itself, not through the narrator's summary.

The 'carrying' motif is the chapter's best structural idea, but it's explained at least three times too many. State it once, in the mother's quoted words. Trust those words to do the work. Cut the passages where Augustine reasons through what the carrying means — let the reader carry it.

Una is mentioned only in the inventory and only as report. Either give her a moment that is dramatized — even a brief specific image Augustine carries of her, something sensory, something that costs him to think — or remove her entirely from this chapter and bring her in when the chapter can afford her the weight she deserves.

The sentence structure throughout is very uniform — the long qualified clause with 'on the [X] of [Y]' construction repeats to the point where it becomes a tic rather than a style. King's prose varies its sentence length aggressively; the shortest sentences in a passage are often where the most weight lands. Look at 'He wept.' That works. More of that.

sections.assessment: This chapter exists almost entirely inside Augustine's skull, and the basilica itself — a North African coastal church in 391 CE, one of the most sensory-rich settings imaginable — barely registers as a physical place. What we get is scaffolding: eastern side aisle, western side aisle, rail, cathedra. These are spatial coordinates, not a world. King would find this a serious deficiency. The smell of incense is never present despite the censer being physically mentioned twice. There are no sound textures beyond spoken Latin and the silence of the congregation — no sandals on stone, no creak of a wooden bench, no harbor noise drifting through the high windows from a port city. No temperature. No light quality. No texture underfoot. The chapter is not without its moments of physical grounding — 'the firm grip of a deacon,' the bad back that makes Valerius lean on the rail, and most powerfully the cold disc at the chapter's end — but these are islands in a sea of abstraction. The prose style is deliberately distanced and institutional, which creates its own kind of momentum, but that momentum is purchased at a steep sensory cost. King's basilica would smell of bodies and lamp oil and old stone and Africa. This one smells of nothing. The setting does almost no double duty: it does not reveal character through its physical details, does not create mood through sensory accumulation, and offers only one genuine 'telling detail' — the disc's temperature — which lands with real force precisely because it is alone.
good_examples: 'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold.' — The chapter's single undeniable telling detail. Cold against the earlier 'disc warm in his hand' at Ostia. The temperature of an object carries the weight of everything the chapter cannot say directly. King-worthy.

'He stood at the rail with his hands on the rail in the way an old bishop stood when his back was bad.' — The bad back is the best physical characterization in the chapter. It earns its place: it shows Valerius's age, his endurance, his embodied authority. One clause does triple duty.

'He did not — and Augustine would, in the years to come, remember this with a particular respect for the deacon's restraint — he did not pull. He held Augustine's arm in the firm grip of a deacon who had been instructed...' — The tactile specificity of the grip vs. the non-pull is precise and good. The reader feels the difference.
bad_examples: 'The censer was brought. The holy oil was brought.' — The two most sensory objects in the entire ordination ritual are handled as inventory. King would have the incense smoke climbing into Augustine's eyes, the oil cold on the crown of his head, the smell of it heavy and ancient. Instead: passive receipt of props.

'The brethren went out of the basilica into the bright morning of the third Sunday of Lent.' — Hippo Regius is a coastal city. 'Bright morning' is everything the outside world gets. No salt smell off the harbor, no heat on stone, no gulls, no sound of a port town. One hundred and forty people just walked out into Africa and the chapter doesn't notice.

'He sat on the bench in the small chamber alone. He sat with the morning.' — The chapter's emotional fulcrum — the moment of solitude after ordination — happens in a room we cannot see or feel or smell. No cold stone bench, no smell of vestment cloth and oil, no quality of the light. The most important room in the chapter is the emptiest.
suggestions: Plant two or three sensory details in the opening paragraph when Augustine enters — not a catalog, a selection: the smell of lamp oil and morning incense, the specific quality of light through high clerestory windows, the low sound of a congregation settling. This does not slow the chapter; it grounds the reader before the trap springs.

Let Hippo Regius exist outside the building. During the ordination or immediately after, let one detail from the port city intrude — a gull, the faint smell of the harbor, the heat beginning to build through the walls. The exterior world pressing in makes the interior transformation feel real.

The ordination ritual is the chapter's climax and it is almost entirely abstract. Valerius's hands on Augustine's head — are they heavy? Dry? Trembling? The holy oil — cold? Warm from the censer? The smell of the incense smoke the censer produces? One physical sentence here would make the moment land harder than any interior monologue can.

The small chamber behind the altar needs one concrete detail: the smell of stored linen, the cold of the stone bench, the stillness of the air versus the nave. Augustine sits with 'the morning' — but the morning needs a texture if the reader is to sit there with him.

The 'cold disc' ending is your best move — protect it by contrast. If you give us one warm or heated sensory detail during the ordination (the oil, the censer, the press of bodies), the cold disc on a shelf in a stone room lands with much more force.

sections.assessment: This chapter operates as a precision-engineered ambush — and it mostly succeeds. The structural trap is beautifully designed: Augustine walks into the basilica thinking he is the helper, and the chapter slowly, methodically reveals that he is the prey. The crowd-as-organism sequence is genuinely unsettling, and the final two sentences — 'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold. He set it back.' — are a near-perfect horror beat buried inside historical fiction, the kind of quiet line King would envy. However, the chapter's greatest weakness is also its most deliberate stylistic choice: the prose operates at a single, uniform register of grave deliberateness from the first sentence to the last, which means there are no true peaks of terror, no valleys of false safety. King builds tension through contrast — the mundane against the monstrous, the fast sentence against the slow paragraph, the laugh before the scream. Here, everything is equally weighted, equally solemn, equally measured, and that flattening ultimately bleeds dread from the moments that most deserve it. The crowd physically taking hold of Augustine and moving him toward the rail is potentially one of the most frightening passages in recent historical fiction — the horror of being moved by a collective will you cannot resist — and the prose handles it with the same analytical calm it used to describe the deacon's bad Latin. The bronze box and the disc are exquisite thriller machinery, but they are underworked here: we get one cold temperature check and the chapter ends. King would have found a second or third beat for the disc — a temperature when Augustine first touched it that day, a flicker of warmth during the ordination, cold at the end — to make the final reveal land with full weight.
good_examples: 'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold. He set it back. He closed the box.' — Three sentences doing the work of a horror chapter's final image. The understatement is perfect. King's rule: the scariest things are said quietly. This is quietly terrifying.

'They moved Augustine forward. They moved him through the side-aisle press to the central aisle. They moved him up the central aisle to the rail. He did not, in the moving, resist.' — The grammatical repetition of 'They moved' transforms the congregation into a single body. The passivity of Augustine — not fighting, not choosing, just being carried — is viscerally disturbing. This is how a crowd becomes a monster.

'Tigisius pointed across the nave. He pointed at Augustine.' — The double-beat of two short sentences creates a slow-motion moment of exposure. One sentence names the gesture. The second sentence lands the target. Correct instinct on the line break.

'the same Tigisius who would, six years later, bring the news to Augustine in his own cell at the bishop's residence about the Donatist preacher named Crispus who had begun to be heard in the harbour district' — Proleptic narration used well: we're told Tigisius matters in a future we haven't reached. The future exists. Something is coming. This is how dread works across time.
bad_examples: 'The bishop of Hippo was Valerius. He was Greek by birth and had been at Hippo in the chair for nine years. His Latin was the Latin of a man who had learned it in his thirties. His preaching was, on Augustine's report from the freedman...' — This is a full paragraph of diocesan biography delivered at the exact moment we should be settling into our seats before the ambush. It dissipates tension rather than building it. The reader doesn't yet know they need to care about Valerius. King would have seeded this information earlier, or cut it to a single charged sentence — enough to know the bishop is old and limited — and saved the real characterization for when Valerius looks Augustine in the eye.

'He thought about the writing he was doing on the matter of the will and the matter of the soul and the matter of the music. He thought about his son, who had died in the late summer of 389 at Tagaste at the age of seventeen...' — This is the chapter's biggest structural misstep. Augustine's interior monologue about his dead son, about Una, about the monastery, runs for six long paragraphs inserted in the middle of the crowd carrying him toward the rail. The crowd has his arm. The congregation is on its feet. And we stop to do backstory. King would never do this. You do not interrupt a scare for an information dump. These paragraphs belong earlier, before the mass begins, as the setup that makes the ambush more devastating.

'He wept. He did not weep loudly. He wept the way a man of thirty-six wept who had walked into a basilica an hour before to attend a Lenten mass and was now, by the formula of the African rite, a presbyter of a diocese he had not been planning to live in.' — The weeping is earned and the image is right, but the explanatory sentence that follows ('the weeping the brethren had not, on Augustine's reading of their faces...') immediately intellectualizes the emotional break. King never explains the weeping. He makes you feel it. Here, the narrator steps in front of the moment and annotates it before the reader can inhabit it.

'He thought of the freedman at the side aisle. The freedman had come to Hippo to ask Augustine about the small final decisions of his own life...' — The chapter's final section loses tension almost entirely by returning to logistical management: the lay brother, the letter to Tagaste, the senior brother. We are winding down through administrative details when the disc — cold, waiting — should be pulling us forward. The dread should be building in this quiet aftermath, not dissolving.
suggestions: Put the backstory about Adeodatus, Una, and the monastery in the first scene — before Augustine enters the basilica. The walk to church in the morning is the place for this material. Then when the crowd takes him, we already carry the grief. The interior monologue in the middle of the ordination sequence must go.

Vary the sentence length deliberately at key moments. The prose is built on long, subordinated, measured sentences — which is the right voice for this narrator — but a short sentence hits differently when everything around it is long. 'The disc was cold.' already works this way. Find three more moments — the pointing finger, the first hand on Augustine's arm, the moment Valerius says his name — and strip those sentences down to bone.

Give the bronze box and the disc a second temperature moment. Let Augustine touch the box when he opens his writing chest at Tagaste the night before he rides to Hippo. Let it be warm — or neutral. Establish a baseline. Then the cold at the end means something specific. Dread is built through contrast. Right now we don't know if cold is wrong because we have no prior data.

Use the ordinary congregation more as a dread instrument before Tigisius stands. Give us one small detail — a woman adjusting her headscarf, a child falling asleep against a column, the smell of the incense — that makes the basilica feel like a real, unremarkable Sunday morning. The horror of what follows is proportional to how fully we believe in the mundane world it destroys.

The crowd moving Augustine is your best moment and it needs one more beat of physical sensation. Not description of his feelings — sensation. The warmth of the hands through the wool of his tunic. The shuffle of sandals on stone. The smell of bodies. Something the body notices before the mind does. That is where dread lives.

Consider ending the chapter at 'He went to compline.' and cutting the three preceding paragraphs of logistics. The disc is cold. He closes the box. He goes to prayers. Full stop. The reader should carry the cold disc into the next chapter, not a solved administrative situation.

sections.assessment: This chapter operates almost entirely in narrative summary and interior monologue, with actual spoken dialogue reduced to perhaps fifteen lines across three thousand words. That's a deliberate formal choice — the Latinate, liturgical register earns its keep — but it means the chapter nearly fails this category on volume alone before King even gets to quality. What dialogue exists lives in two registers: the ceremonial (Valerius at the cathedra, Tigisius at the rail) and the intimate (the freedman at the door). The ceremonial dialogue succeeds structurally — the call-and-response of the ordination exchange is compressed to the point of ritual weight, and that compression does real emotional work. But the intimate dialogue is where the writer actually finds a voice, and there's far too little of it. The deepest wound is Tigisius's naming speech, which is a textbook 'as you know Bob' dump: the congregation at Hippo did not need a deacon to tell them Augustine was from Tagaste, had been three months back from Milan, and had founded a monastic house. The author needed them to know, so Tigisius tells them. King would cut that speech to a single gesture — the pointed finger — and let the narrative carry the credentials. The best dialogue in the chapter is two words: 'And you.' The freedman doesn't ask what carrying means. The freedman doesn't fill the silence. That restraint is the most King-like moment in the piece, and it arrives in the last quarter after the chapter has already spent its dramatic capital. The voices are largely indistinct from one another — Valerius, Tigisius, and Augustine all speak in the same complete, formal, subordinate-clause-heavy sentences — with only the freedman's brevity marking a different human frequency.
good_examples: 'Aurelius Augustinus. / Father. / The brethren have brought you to the rail. / They have. / The brethren have named you for the priesthood of the diocese of Hippo. / They have. / Do you accept the call.' — The compression here is surgical. Five exchanges, twenty-nine words, and the weight of the whole chapter lands on them. The lack of question marks on 'Do you accept the call' is exactly right — it reads as statement, not interrogative, because both men know it is.

'Aurelius. The brothers will manage.' / 'And you.' — The freedman's two-word follow-up is the best line in the chapter. It carries everything not said: I know you are breaking, I know you are afraid, I am not going to ask you the questions you cannot answer. This is subtext working exactly as King means it. The writer earns it by having the freedman stay silent about 'the carrying' — the narrative then explains why, but the silence in the dialogue itself does the heavier lifting.
bad_examples: 'The man who is on the eastern side aisle in the dark tunic with the lay brother of his household at his side is Aurelius Augustinus of Tagaste, who has been three months in Africa from his return from Milan and who has founded the small monastic house at Tagaste...' — This is 'as you know Bob' on parchment. No deacon in a congregation of a hundred and forty people addresses his church like a legal dossier. The author needed Augustine's credentials on the table; Tigisius delivers them to the room for the reader's benefit, not the congregation's. King would have Tigisius point and say one sentence, then let the narrative do the credential work in the next paragraph.

'The plan has changed. I will not be returning to Tagaste with you. I will be at Hippo from this week. The lay brother will ride with you tomorrow back to Tagaste. He will take a sealed letter to the senior brother. The letter will contain instructions about the house. Tell the senior brother I will write him fully in the next month. Tell the senior brother also that I am sorry.' — Augustine's speech to the freedman is a logistical monologue with no seams, no hesitation, no break. It reads like dictation. A man who has just been ordained against his will and is now dissolving his life in real time would not deliver clean compound sentences in correct order. One stutter. One restart. One sentence that trails. The formal register of the chapter cannot excuse a man speaking with no body in him.
suggestions: Cut Tigisius's naming speech to its emotional core — the pointed finger and a single declarative — and move Augustine's credentials into the narrative summary that precedes it. 'The man in the eastern aisle is the one we have been waiting for' does more work and sounds like a deacon, not a legal brief.

Give Valerius one verbal tic or construction that marks his Greek-inflected Latin as different from African Latin — a reversed word order, a phrase that lands slightly off, something that makes the congregation's patience audible. Currently we are told his Latin is the Latin of a man who learned it in his thirties; we never hear it.

Break Augustine's speech to the freedman into actual exchange. Let the freedman interrupt once. Let Augustine not finish a sentence. The freedman knows Augustine well enough to speak across him — that intimacy is established and then not used where it would matter most.

The mother's words at Ostia — 'I have been the watcher. You will be the carrier.' — are the best-crafted spoken sentences in the chapter, and they're in memory, not scene. Consider whether one more fragment of her voice, handled with the same compression as the ordination exchange, could appear at the moment Augustine says yes. Not explanation. One line of hers in his head at the rail, placed where it lands like a fist.

sections.assessment: This chapter contains some of the most formally controlled historical prose in recent fiction, and therein lies both its strength and its central structural problem. The author is working with a genuinely cinematic scene — an involuntary ordination by mob consensus — and the bones are right: setup, crisis, aftermath. But the ratio is badly skewed. The setup runs three full pages before Tigisius stands, and those pages move at a single, unvarying pace — the same stately cadence regardless of whether we're learning why Augustine came to Hippo or watching a congregation rise to its feet. King's great insight about pace is that it should be *felt* — the reader's pulse should quicken and slow with the prose's physical rhythm. Here, the liturgical sameness of the sentence structure becomes anesthetic. When the crisis finally erupts, it partially rescues itself: the crowd scene has genuine forward momentum, and the compression of the ordination itself — after all that interior preparation — is sophisticated. But the author then makes a critical error by stopping the clock during the highest-tension moment (Augustine at the rail) to explain the thriller MacGuffin ('the thing in the box'). That's not tension; that's the author jumping in front of his own scene. The aftermath — the bench, the freedman, the empty nave — earns its quieter pace, and the final cold-disc sentence is exactly right. The chapter knows how to end. It doesn't always know how to move.
good_examples: 'He wept.' — A two-word paragraph after a long, formal ordination sequence. The white space does exactly what King means when he talks about letting the sentence breathe. Everything that comes before that sentence earns this one.

'He had not thought about Hippo.' — A perfectly placed paragraph-as-pivot. Short declarative sentence alone on the page, at the moment of maximum irony. The reader lands on it like a step missing in the dark. This is pacing by structure.

'Father. I accept.' — The capitulation that drives the entire chapter is given two sentences and a line break. The author correctly understands that the biggest moment in the scene should take the least space. Everything before it is the compression spring; this is the release.

'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold.' — Final sentence. Thriller element reintroduced with a single detail. No explanation. King would approve: you earn the reader's imagination and then deploy it.
bad_examples: 'He did not move because the body of a man at thirty-six who had been pointed at across the nave of a basilica he had not been baptised in does not, in the first count, move.' — This is the shock moment, and it deserves three short sentences, not one long subordinate-clause burial. King would write: 'He did not move. A man pointed at across a nave does not move. Not at first.' The reader needs to feel the freeze, not parse the grammar.

The entire second paragraph ('The man was a friend of his at Tagaste...') running nine sentences of pure backstory before anything happens. King loads his backstory on the move — give us one line of why Augustine is at Hippo, then let us follow him through the door. We don't need the full provenance of the freedman's decision before we've cared about any character in the scene.

'He understood, at the rail, that the call was not a call he could refuse... the small bronze box... the carrying of the thing in the box... a presbyter would, on the working of the African church's promotion patterns, become a bishop in some not many years.' — This is three paragraphs of exposition dropped into the scene's tensest moment. The congregation is on its feet. The bishop is watching. And the author pauses to explain the MacGuffin and future career trajectory. Cut all of this. It belongs in the bench scene afterward, when the crisis is over and interiority is earned.

'The Lenten readings were the readings proper to the third Sunday. The cantor was the cantor. The deacons brought the elements forward at the offertory.' — The liturgical shorthand is intentional and could work, but 'the cantor was the cantor' is not compression — it's empty filler dressed as style. It takes up line space without delivering anything. King would cut it.
suggestions: Cut the backstory paragraph in half and move the rest into Augustine's post-ordination reflection on the bench, where interiority is structurally appropriate. Open with one fact — he came to Hippo for a friend — and put us inside the basilica by sentence four.

When Tigisius stands and the congregation rises, shorten every sentence by half. 'Tigisius pointed across the nave. He pointed at Augustine.' Good. But then: 'The eastern side aisle was on its feet. The western side aisle was on its feet. The whole nave was on its feet.' Three separate sentences as three separate shocks — not one combined statement. The crowd should feel like a wave hitting the reader.

Remove the 'thing in the box' and 'promotion patterns' exposition entirely from the rail scene. At the rail, Augustine's interior experience should be emotional and immediate — the memory of his mother's words earns its place; the MacGuffin inventory does not. Save the bronze-box reckoning for the bench scene where he is alone and the chapter's pace has room for it.

Vary the sentence opener pattern in the early setup. The chapter runs fifteen consecutive sentences beginning with 'He' or 'The' in the first two pages. King watches for He-stacks and cuts them not for grammatical reasons but for rhythmic ones — the reader goes numb to a noun that never changes.

The small parlour sentence at the chapter's close ('He went to the small parlour at the bishop's residence to take the noon meal with Valerius, who would, in the four years between the present hour...') is doing too much. The chapter has already ended emotionally with 'The disc was cold.' That final expository sentence about Valerius's death and the writing room's future undercuts the image. Trust the cold disc. End there.

sections.assessment: The chapter carries genuine thematic weight, and most of it earns its keep. The central tension—between the life a man plans and the life he is given—arises entirely from the specific situation: Augustine comes to Hippo for a friend's sake and leaves as someone else's priest. That's the story doing the thematic work, not the author. The 'carrying' motif, tied to the mysterious disc and to the mother's prophecy at Ostia, functions as a throughline for vocation as burden rather than gift—a distinction King would approve of, because it refuses to sentimentalize the call. The secondary thematic layer—the grief over Adeodatus, the unresolved silence with Una—operates beautifully beneath the institutional drama without ever surfacing as the point. Augustine is being pulled forward into a public life while dragging unresolved private wreckage behind him. The author trusts us to feel that without stating it. The Lenten setting (preparation, sacrifice, the liturgical calendar as destiny) works without strain. Where the chapter stumbles is in the long interior passages where Augustine explicates the 'carrying' to himself—and therefore to us—with more precision than the subtext can survive. King's rule is that theme is what the story is about when the characters aren't explaining it. Several passages here cross that line: Augustine at the rail essentially diagrams the symbolic architecture of his own life, and the diagram is more explicit than the symbol needs to be. The final paragraphs slide from fiction into chronicle, which breaks the thematic spell precisely where it should be deepest.
good_examples: 'He did not pull. He held Augustine's arm in the firm grip of a deacon who had been instructed, by some further discernment Augustine could not, in the moment, account for, that the man at the eastern side aisle would come if he was held and would not require pulling.' — Theme as action: the nature of divine calling rendered in physical restraint. No gloss needed.

'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold. / He set it back. He closed the box. He returned the box to the upper shelf. He went to compline.' — Eight words of symbol followed by the normalcy of liturgical routine. King-level understatement. The cold disc means everything and the prose refuses to say what.

'The weeping was, on his own honest accounting, the weeping of a man who had, in the last quarter of an hour, understood that his life was no longer the life he had been planning to live.' — The theme of vocational surrender stated through the body's honest report, not through authorial instruction. The subtext stays under the surface because it's anchored in sensation.

'I have been the watcher. You will be the carrier. The carrying is the work. There is no other work that comes with it.' — Prophecy that doesn't explain itself. The mother's four sentences are the thematic spine of the entire chapter and they work because they refuse elaboration.
bad_examples: 'He understood, at the rail, that the carrying his mother had given him at Ostia had been, on her own honest accounting, the carrying of the thing in the box; and that the carrying of the thing in the box had been, on her implicit instruction which he had not, in three years and four months, finished hearing, the carrying of whatever further work the office of a man of his disposition would require him to take, in the years to come, in order that the thing in the box would be in the hands of a man who could carry it as the thing in the box required to be carried.' — The motif collapses under its own repetition. 'Carrying' appears eight times in a single sentence. King's test: if you need this many words to explain what your symbol means, the symbol isn't working yet. Cut to the bone. The cold disc at the end does in eight words what this paragraph fails to do in eighty.

'The thing in the box required, on the long mapping of the empire and the church and the diocese of Hippo and the hundred and forty Christians on their feet behind him in the nave, to be in the hands of a bishop.' — The author is diagramming the plot's symbolic logic for us. Augustine wouldn't think in these terms; this is the writer anxious that we've missed the point. Trust the scene. The hundred and forty people on their feet are already doing this work.

'Valerius had gone to the small parlour at the bishop's residence next door for the noon meal... who would, in those four years, watch Augustine grow into the office at the slow methodical rate of a Greek of sixty-six who had been at Hippo nine years and was prepared to give the new presbyter from Tagaste the time the new presbyter required to become the presbyter the diocese had been needing.' — The chapter ends in historiography. The thematic register drops from fiction to chronicle precisely where it should deepen. The cold disc closes the chapter perfectly; this paragraph, which follows it in the chapter's structural logic, undoes that closing by explaining the future rather than inhabiting the present.
suggestions: Gut the interior-monologue explication of the 'carrying' at the rail. Keep the first 'He understood' sentence, then cut to 'He looked at Valerius.' The reader has the mother's words; they don't need Augustine to diagram what those words mean across three paragraphs. The symbol earns its keep or it doesn't—and it does, if you let it.

Move the cold-disc sentence to the final line of the chapter and cut everything after 'He went to compline.' The historiographical summary of Valerius's tenure is a tonal misfire that breaks the thematic spell. End on the cold. That's the theme. That's the subtext. That's the whole chapter in eight words.

The Una subtext is the chapter's strongest buried layer—develop it one beat further without surfacing it. A single physical detail from the bench scene (a gesture, a smell, a sound from outside that triggers her) would give that grief more thematic anchor without naming it. King calls this 'the thing you don't say': one more careful not-saying of Una would make her absence resonate louder than any mention.

The Lenten setting is doing real thematic work (sacrifice, preparation, the season of becoming) but it's almost entirely calendrical—'third Sunday of Lent' appears three times as a timestamp. Consider one sensory detail of Lent (ash, fasting light, the particular quality of penitential air in a basilica) that lets the season's thematic meaning bleed into the scene without announcement.

sections.assessment: This chapter earns its period setting through institutional and political specificity rather than sensory atmosphere — a legitimate and sophisticated approach to 391 CE North Africa, but one that creates an uneven result when judged by King's standard. The author clearly knows this world cold: the Donatist schism named and geographically placed, the Greek bishop's linguistic limitation rendered precisely, the African church's custom of irregular ordination by congregational acclamation, the liturgical structure of a Lenten mass, the cathedra and choir and the deacon's proper place at various rites. These are not generic 'ancient church' trappings — they are the specific institutional furniture of a specific diocese in a specific contested region in the late fourth century. Where the chapter falls short of King's ideal is in the sensory and material layer. We are told it is a bright morning in North Africa in early spring — and that is essentially all the physical world gives us. King's cultural grounding works because it gives you the brand name on the radio, the smell of the diner, the song you can actually hear. Here we get 'the censer,' 'the holy oil,' 'the noon meal' — named but not inhabited. What does the basilica smell like at the third hour? What does the African congregation wear? What does the harbour town of Hippo sound like bleeding through the walls when a hundred and forty people go still? The political and ecclesiastical texture is first-rate. The lived, sensory texture of 391 CE coastal North Africa is almost entirely absent. The prose style — deliberately stripped, McCarthy-adjacent in its repetition — makes this a conscious choice rather than an oversight, but the choice has a cost: we are reconstructing the period rather than inhabiting it.
good_examples: 'His Latin was the Latin of a man who had learned it in his thirties. His preaching was, on Augustine's report from the freedman who had been at Hippo for the previous year, the preaching of a man who had something to say and had, on the limits of his Latin, the recurring difficulty of finding the African ear for it.' — This is period-specific in the most King-like way: not a generic 'foreign accent' but a precise rendering of what linguistic acculturation meant in a world where Latin itself was fragmenting into regional dialects, and where an African congregation had its own ear.

'the Donatists in the next valley or from the imperial administration at Carthage' — Six words and you are in the specific political geography of late Roman North Africa: the schismatic Christian movement with real territorial presence, the imperial bureaucracy in the regional capital. Nothing generic about this. King would approve.

'the same Tigisius who would, six years later, bring the news to Augustine in his own cell at the bishop's residence about the Donatist preacher named Crispus who had begun to be heard in the harbour district' — Naming the heretic, naming the deacon, placing the heretic in a specific district of a specific port town. This is how you anchor a world.

'the African congregation said the responses in the African Latin that was their own' — The linguistic stratification of the congregation is an entire social history in one clause. Valerius's Greek-accented Latin against the African vernacular responses: this is the empire at the moment of fracture, rendered without commentary.
bad_examples: 'the candles at the altar were burning down' and 'the bright morning' — We get two weather or atmosphere details in the entire scene and both are interchangeable with any church in any century. What does morning light do in a North African basilica with high windows? What does the sea smell like four blocks away? King would not let a specific coastal city go unfelt.

'He went to the small parlour at the bishop's residence to take the noon meal with Valerius' — The noon meal is mentioned twice and we learn nothing about it. Food is one of King's primary period anchors. What does a Greek bishop in a small African diocese eat at noon in 391? This is a missed beat.

'They took the new presbyter's vestments off him and folded them.' — Vestments, folded. The material culture of the ordination rite — the weight and texture of the cloth, the specific color, what it looked like on a man of thirty-six who had not dressed for the priesthood that morning — is entirely absent. The ritual is procedurally correct but physically invisible.

'a man in the dark tunic' — This is the only clothing detail we receive for Augustine across the entire chapter. A hundred and forty North African Christians in a basilica in 391 and we get one dark tunic. King would have us knowing what the freedman was wearing, what Tigisius's layman's dress told us about his rank, what the congregation's dress told us about the economic range of the diocese.
suggestions: Give the basilica a physical body in the opening. One specific sensory detail at the moment Augustine enters — the quality of the light through the clerestory windows, the smell of the incense and the crowd in the third hour, the sound of the African Latin responses reverberating off stone — would anchor every institutional detail that follows. You've earned the right to one good sentence of sensory grounding.

The 'bright morning' as Augustine leaves the vestry chamber and stands in the empty nave is a loaded moment and you spend none of it on the physical world. Hippo is a port. The morning outside would have sound. Even one specific sensory intrusion — gulls, the smell of the harbour two streets over, the sound of carts on the stone — would make the nave feel like a real place inside a real city rather than a stage set.

The noon meal with Valerius is a closing scene beat. Give it one period-specific detail: what is on the table, what does the bishop's residence smell like, what is the quality of the silence between two men who have just done an extraordinary thing in a small room together. The institutional relationship is established; the material world is not.

Consider the congregation as period-specific class texture, not only as liturgical body. A small coastal diocese in Roman Africa in 391 would contain freedmen, small merchants, sailors, possibly a decurion or two, possibly enslaved persons in the back. You have already established the freedman and the lay brother. One more specific social-class detail — even a sentence — would make the hundred and forty feel like a cross-section of a real late antique African port rather than a generic Christian congregation.

The Donatist detail about Crispus and the harbour district is one of the best cultural moments in the chapter. Consider doing for the material world of Hippo what you've done for its ecclesiastical politics: name one more specific thing about the city's physical identity as a place, not just its institutional one.

sections.assessment: This chapter is doing something deliberate and ambitious — a liturgical, incantatory rhythm meant to echo Augustine's own Confessions. That intention is real and it earns some of what it tries. But viewed through King's lens, the prose is not invisible. You feel the writing constantly. The central problem is a cluster of interlocking tics that compound: 'on the [noun] of [noun]' constructions appear upward of twenty times ('on Augustine's report from the freedman,' 'on the freedman's report,' 'on the long custom of the African church,' 'on his honest accounting,' 'on the present trajectory of the house'); the word 'small' saturates the page like a verbal nervous habit ('small slow approaches,' 'small monastic house,' 'small final decisions,' 'small habitual marking,' 'small still part,' 'small bronze box,' 'small writing chamber,' 'small Catholic cemetery,' 'small coastal diocese,' 'small house' — twelve or more instances); and the chapter opens with a full page of backstory that delays the story. King's 2nd-draft rule — first draft minus ten percent — would go straight for these. But there are genuine King moments buried here: the three short punches ('He wept.' 'He was at the rail.' 'He had not thought about Hippo.') are exactly right. The ending is quiet and cold and correct. The mother's four-sentence speech lands. The congregation rising aisle by aisle builds with real menace. The bones are strong. The fat is significant.
good_examples: 'He wept.' followed eventually by 'He wept until the rite was finished.' — King compression at its best: the short declarative drops you into the emotion, then the parallel close delivers the weight without editorial. Nothing explained, nothing underlined.
'He had not thought about Hippo.' — After the long interiority of the backstory paragraphs, this seven-word sentence is the whole paragraph. It earns its place by contrast. King would approve.

'The disc, when Augustine checked it on the evening it arrived, was cold.' — Perfect chapter close. The mystery object, the anticlimax, the single adjective. This is the sentence that makes the reader turn back to the top of the chapter and wonder what they missed.

'I have been the watcher. You will be the carrier. The carrying is the work. There is no other work that comes with it.' — Four short declaratives. No adverbs, no hedging. The mother's voice is definite in a chapter full of qualification. The rhythm does all the emotional work.

'The whole eastern side aisle was on its feet. The whole western side aisle was on its feet. The whole congregation in the centre of the nave was on its feet.' — Controlled, intentional repetition used the way King uses it: to let the physical reality accumulate until it becomes pressure.
bad_examples: 'The man was a friend of his at Tagaste, a freedman who had been making, for the better part of a year, the small slow approaches of a man who was thinking about leaving the world for the small monastic house Augustine had founded on the family property at Tagaste in the year after his return from Italy.' — One sentence doing the work of a paragraph. The nested subordinate clauses ('a freedman who had been making... of a man who was thinking... for the small monastic house...') force the reader to carry too many open parentheses. King would break this into three sentences and cut 'the small' at minimum twice.

'He did not resist because the body of a man at thirty-six who was being moved by a hundred and forty Christians at the third hour of a Sunday morning toward a rail at which an old Greek bishop was standing was not the body of a man who could resist.' — This sentence collapses under its own construction. 'The body of a man at thirty-six who was being moved... was not the body of a man who could resist' is a subordinate clause buried inside a dependent clause buried inside the sentence's main claim. The reader has to backtrack. This is prose calling attention to itself as architecture.

'The deacon was, on Augustine's quick recognition of him, a man named Tigisius — the same Tigisius who would, six years later, bring the news to Augustine in his own cell at the bishop's residence about the Donatist preacher named Crispus who had begun to be heard in the harbour district.' — The forward-flash to Tigisius six years later is an author intrusion. It breaks the present-tense pressure of the ordination scene to show the writer's research. King would cut from 'a man named Tigisius' and nothing after. Let the reader live in 391.

'He had thought about the writing he was doing on the matter of the will and the matter of the soul and the matter of the music.' — 'The matter of X and the matter of Y and the matter of Z' is the 'small' tic in a different dress. Three parallel 'matter of' clauses in one sentence when 'his writing on the will, the soul, the music' does the same work in half the words. The 10% rule would find this and every sentence like it.

'on his own honest accounting' / 'on every accounting of his future life' / 'on the long custom of the African church' / 'on the present trajectory of the house' / 'on the working of the African church's promotion patterns' / 'on the long mapping of the empire' — The 'on the [noun] of [noun]' construction is the chapter's dominant tic, occurring more than twenty times. It reads as a tic not as style because it attaches to nearly every causal or evaluative statement. King's prose is invisible because it doesn't repeat its own architecture at you. This phrase pattern is the prose clearing its throat every third sentence.
suggestions: Do a single-purpose pass with 'Find' for the word 'small.' Mark every instance. Keep three. Cut the rest. If 'small' is pulling the thematic weight of humility and interiority, find a noun or image that does it once — the box, the cell, the lamp — and let that object carry the modesty instead of the adjective.

Run 'on the' through Find and Read every 'on the X of Y' or 'on the X accounting' aloud. If the sentence still means the same thing without the phrase, cut the phrase. Most will survive the surgery: 'on his honest accounting' is almost always just 'honestly' or nothing at all — the context has already made the honesty clear.

The opening page (the backstory of the freedman's decision) reads like a summary of events that happened before the story. Move the chapter's first sentence — 'He had come to Hippo for a man' — to one line, then cut directly to Augustine entering the basilica. The backstory can come in by the second page as interiority during the mass, in single sentences, not the paragraph-long logistics chain that opens the chapter now.

The sentence 'He did not resist because the body of a man at thirty-six...' wants to be two sentences: what he did, then why. 'He didn't resist. A hundred and forty Christians were moving him; there was no body that resisted that.' The subordinate-clause nesting is solving a problem (how to narrate passivity with interiority) that shorter syntax solves faster.

The Tigisius future-flash should be cut entirely from this chapter. The detail about the Donatist preacher Crispus and the news six years later is historical scaffolding, not story. If Tigisius returns in a later chapter, the reader will have been introduced to him here without needing to know his future role. Trust the reader. Trust the future chapter.

overall_assessment: King-style analysis complete. Overall grade: B-.
